speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4. March 9, 1997. The survivor is Albert Roland. Interviewer Toni Katsh Binstock, in Denver, Colorado, United States of America, and the interview is being conducted in English. You were talking about coming back after the war ended. You said you were in Shymkent when the war ended. I wanted to ask you about that. Did, did you have to have papers to go back then? You said the bord-- you thought the borders were going to be opened. Did you need papers to get back into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we came to-- with those trains, with those freight trains--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the light went off. We're going to have to stop the tape now. We had a little technical problem. The light went out. So that's been fixed, and we're going to continue on tape 4 here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were telling us about, uh, coming back into Poland after the, the um, after the war ended. So why don't you just continue your story? You said you were on the trains, and you were coming back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So when we arrived, uh, on the Polish side of the border, some people expressed themself in such a way that it was just a horrible thing to listen if they saw what they, what they did see and they, they still thought that it wasn't enough, you know, killing going on. They said, still so many Jews left. But uh, my idea not to stay in Poland too long, to be again in, in, in such a, a, bad, uh, atmosphere like we had before the war. I was looking already for ways to, to get rid of that place as fast as I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who all came back with you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother. But you asked me before, I just remember, if you needed papers to cross the border from Russia to Poland. Those people from the KGB, I think they were from KGB, they had papers with the names, you know, every car, you know. And they had to present it probably on the border. They were counting us and checking, you know, everybody's name, you know, how many people. And this is how we crossed the border. We didn't need no visas or no legal papers. That was sufficient, you know. Because they probably had an understanding with the Polish government about, you know, how to go about it. So, uh, they took us-- they, that means the train-- took us to Liegnitz. Liegnitz was a part that was Germany, and Poland was taking away from the Germans. Not Poland, the Russians from Poland, because they were already working together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was Silesia already. That was Upper Silesia. No, it's Lower Silesia, I guess. I don't remember exactly. But anyway, it was Liegnitz. And uh, they gave us some quarters over there. And we-- you know, I was together with my future wife's parents, you know, with the family, so we decided to leave the kids in-- my future wife's father and mother, they wanted to see Warsaw, because they heard that there was a, a fight, you know, in the Warsaw ghetto, you know, so on and so forth. And since where they lived it used to be in the area where the Jewish ghetto was, so they were eager to see, you know, what happened, how things are over there. So we took a train. We could already free, you know, take a train and, and, and travel. And good enough, we went to, to Warsaw with them. But Warsaw was so bombed out, it was-- everything was in rubbles. The whole area where the Jewish ghetto was, there was seldom a building standing untouched. Because the, the Germans was, were bombing just left and right. It was nothing. You couldn't see a street, you couldn't see nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow my father, my future father-in-law, he got pretty good orientation. He found a spot where the building was standing, you know. So there was not much, you know, to see and not much to, to hope about over there. And then we decided we're going to go to Lodz, where I lived, and, and see, you know, what's happening over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Lodz, and, and, and they had a curfew until 7:00 o'clock. You could-- after 7:00, you are not supposed to be on the street. Any Jew or any person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any person. So we came, and we didn't have too much time to find anybody and to see anybody, but we found out where the Jewish committ-- committee was-- the address of the Jewish committee. So since I knew Lodz, you know, very well, I was born and raised there, so we all went to, to to see, maybe, the office is still open, even though it was already late in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came over there, and nobody was there. All the offices were closed. And where we go from here? We didn't know. And there's 7:00 o'clock almost on the nose, and we didn't know what to do. So yeah, in the meantime, yeah, on the way-- I forgot to tell you-- on the way back to Poland on a railroad station, you know, I found a cousin of mine. He was also in Russia. It was an old-- my father's brother's son. His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told us that he heard that a, a sister of his survived the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was with his wife already. He was married already. And he gave us the address of, uh, where his sister lives, because somehow he could obtain an address where she lived. And somehow, I mixed up, because the, the streets were, were changed, you know the- the names a little bit, and the numbers. Anyway, when I came to Lodz, I went to, to see if, if, you know, what I understood where the address is, and she was living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, after I came to the-- the, to the others of the Jewish committee, we were standing there debating where to go. No. There was nobody that I knew, you know, where people live or what happened. So I was standing there. In the meantime, I heard voices from the top floor, voices. So I was so eager to see who those people are, so I started climbing the steps, going up, one, one story, the next story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, on the third floor, which was a top floor, I saw people coming down. They were Jewish people. So I see, I look at faces, if I recognize anybody. One person, another person, I don't see nobody. And all of a sudden, like a, a miracle, somebody showed up that I knew from school days. He was so happy to see me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and I was happy to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. And he, he was, he was already married. I think he was in Russia, too. I don't remember exactly. I don't remember exactly. And he had an uncle that was already in Lodz that survived the, the, the concentration camp, and he had an apartment. So he stayed-- his uncle was away-- so he stayed in his uncle's apartment with his wife. So he took us over there. In the meantime, I could find out here and there, you know, put pieces together. You know, because he was already quite a few days over there, so he met people. He met one person, another person, and he-- I think that he saw my cousin, and he knew the address that they were living now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we slept overnight there, you know. And the next morning, we went out again on the search, you know, so I can find my cousin. And I was going on the same street where they were supposed to live, but I don't, I don't remember whether I knew the, the house, the add-- the, the number of the house. But walking down the street, I heard a voice behind me, and she was calling me. My cousin was calling me, Abramic! I turn around, there she is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was right in front of her house, where she lived. So I went up there, and we were so happy to see each other. And her brother already was there, you know. So we spent a couple of days. And I didn't want to stay in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go try to find your home and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said that the house, because she went there, they were, they were staying there in the ghetto in our apartment, my cousin. In the ghetto, they were staying, three families, in the two rooms there were three families staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where you lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: An uncle of mine, the older brother of my father's, and, and they were the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Po-- where you lived, they made it the ghetto? It was part of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where we lived, yeah, they made the ghetto. Yeah. So she told us that she went there, and the building is all in ruins. Not-- the outside walls was still standing, but since people didn't have-- the Gentile people, the Polish people didn't have wood to, to heat up the houses in the wintertime, so when they took the people out and they sent them away to the concentration camps to, for, for, you know, to kill them, so they went in there. They tear up the floors and everything, and the, the, the the doors, everything they took out, and the window frames. Everything they took out, they broke out. So I figure, why, why should I go there? There's nothing for me to see, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with my cousin a couple days and my future in-laws, and we decided to go back to the kids, you know. The kids were left alone in Liegnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let me ask you, did you, did you find out what happened to your mother? Did you think she'd gone to a concentration camp, or did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We, I don't remember when it was. My brother and I, we wrote a letter. I had, uh, my mother's sister, my aunt, in Switzerland, that I told you, and we wrote a letter to them. They answered that they found out through the Red Cross that our mother died from starvation. She left. You know, when-- before they closed the ghetto, they had a chance to, to leave, you know. So they went-- she and a, and a, a neighbor, her husband and her son, left also for Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wanted to go. They thought maybe they're going to find us, find us. So they came as, as close as the, the [POLISH] you know, in a town named Siedlce. They couldn't go any farther. They didn't have money to pay, you know, so they'll take them across the river.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they stayed there, and when in '41 the war started, you know, they didn't have no food and they, they died from starvation. And I didn't know whether she was buried or whatever, you know. So there was nothing that I can inquire. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your older brother, Avram? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aaron, I mean. Aaron. Did you try to find him there or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Henry, Henry was-- he left Russia together with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the older brother, Aaron, I sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aaron. Yeah. We found out about Aaron, because we wrote a letter to my mother. She was still in, in Lodz. Oh, no, she was already-- she left Lodz. She was in Siedlce. But we, we wrote a letter, and my aunt wrote me the address where she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sent her a couple packages, you know. And my mother wrote us that Aaron also wrote to her, and she got his address. So this is how we got connection with, with Aaron. And Aaron, in the meantime, he, he got into a labor camp, because he was inducted in a, in a labor army. Because foreigners they didn't take in the, into the regular army, just to the labor army where they could help out, you know, to, to dig ditches, you know, on the front line or whatever, you know, they could do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or they did the factories where they need workers because people were taken to the regular army, and they worked in a factory in [NON-ENGLISH], and they had it very tough, very hard, hard work. They were not in a, in a camp. So they were-- I say 'they,' it was my cousin and other friends, you know, that they lived there. They, they, they inducted them, and they thought that they going into the regular army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which army? Which regular army? The Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was in Russia. So they were surprised that they put them in, in the working army, trud army, they called it. 'Trud' is, is working. So they didn't like it. They wanted to go and fight the Germans, you see. So they were looking out to get out, you know, and be free and work again like, like they used to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got together, and they-- that was in a very, very remote place, you know, where they, they used to work in a factory where they made pig iron, you know, and they have to carry the, that pig iron, you know, and load up barges, you know, and stuff like that. My brother was a barber. So anyway, they got together, and they're going to get out of there. They thought it's a joke to get out of there. So they had to go over a river, you know, and without crossing that river, you couldn't leave that place. You see, the Russians knew where to build those, those, those places for forced labor. It wasn't forced labor for them yet, right? So they had to go, and they had a [? pram. ?] You know, you know what a [? pram ?] is? It's like, like a, a platform to take over, uh, the stuff on the, on the other side, you know, like, like heavy machinery or stuff like that, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for that, there was an operator, you know, with a motor, and it went with the line, you know, across. So they had to go to that operator. And, I mean, that operator was working with the KGB. So he called up, and he said, we got, oh, five or six people, you know, that are trying to run away, you know. So the KGB came, and they, they put them in, in a slave labor camp. And they had to do the same kind of work, you know, but under, under a gun, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after three and a half years, it's a good thing he was a barber. He wouldn't survive. It was such a hard work, terrible. But somebody found out from the, from the, the guards, you know, that he's a barber, so he, he shaved him, and he cut his hair, and then somebody higher found out, you know. So he had already a little bit, you know-- he knew the right people, you know. So he had it-- he became officially a barber over there in that camp. And who did he cut hair? You know, all these people, the big, big shots, you know. So this is how he survived. So he got to know a girl over there, and they got married later on. It was Maged was the other, the other woman. She was from Lithuania, that woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we wrote to them, you know, he found out where we are. He got our address. After the release from the labor camp, he came to us with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they come to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was in, in, in [NON-ENGLISH]. Now they call it the old name, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. It's [NON-ENGLISH]. So he came to us over there to [NON-ENGLISH], and he worked as a barber. And in the meantime, we were already-- no, not to [NON-ENGLISH]. He came to [NON-ENGLISH]. He came to [NON-ENGLISH]. Yeah. Later, he was, he was in, in that camp inducted. Yeah. And then he found out where we are in [NON-ENGLISH], so then he came to [NON-ENGLISH], and he worked as a barber, too. So in the-- he, well, he had-- in the meantime, he had a child with that, with that woman. So he was-- it was a little bit different for him. But with this woman, this woman was the first wife he had a child. I'm mixing up a little bit. With his first wife, he had a child, before he got inducted. So then he got to know this woman, and he got married with her, and he came to us to [NON-ENGLISH] also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew what had happened to him, and you found out what happened to Mother. And then you were telling us before about so you were in Lodz. And then finish about your story about from Lodz when you decided to leave there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Lodz we went back to Liegnitz, because the kids were in Liegnitz, in, in, Upper-- Lower Silesia. And then we were thinking that, uh, how to go away from Poland. So in the meantime, they were creating, organizing kibbutz. So with the kibbutz, they could go to, to Israel, which was at that time Palestine. But they were preparing already-- they were called the Bricha-- the Zionist organization-- Bricha. They were doing a very efficient job to organize people and young people, you know, to go to Israel before it was Israel, you know. And so we had the chance to go with, with them to, to leave Liegnitz. No, we stayed in Liegnitz a short time. We were working the kibbutz, you know. I had a job that I worked, you know. And this is how we, everybody went to work and brought a check, you know. And this is how, you know, money to buy food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the kibbutz liquidated, we all went across the border to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia, we were not supposed to say that we are from Poland. We were-- we had to say that we are Greeks. We are going home to Greek-- to Greece. And when the Russians supposed to ask us questions, we don't know Russian. We don't know Polish. We are Greeks, you know. And it worked. So we came to Austria. We came to Austria, in [NON-ENGLISH], but my future wife's parents, she went-- they went to in different kibbutz for grown-up people. We were for youngsters, you know. And her brother was in the children's kibbutz. So when we left Poland, they went-- no, we went before-- no, the parents went before us. And we went secondary. And the boy went with another kibbutz, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, we took-- they took us to Ebensee. Ebensee was a, a, a concentration camp, was like Auschwitz. They had ovens and gas chambers, too. So they were killing Jews in a wholesale way, you know. And they wanted [INAUDIBLE] to work with, with, uh, you know, to, to break rock, rocks, you know, very, very hard labor. And, and one of the inhabitants that came with us, they found a dead person underneath the floor. There were loose, loose floors, loose boards, you know, because people were trying, when they took them for selections, to try to hide. So they try to, to loosen up the boards, and they were hiding under the floor. And then this is how they died, because they, they were already weak enough to that they couldn't even get out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to protest. We wanted to get out of that place. Because it was a hor-- such a horrible place to be. Who was running it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was running it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: UNRRA was run--

speaker: interviewee
text: UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was running-- they put you in, into Ebensee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they needed to, yeah. So they had another camp, you know, not, not too far. They put us in this camp. And there I found some, some friend from Lodz, you know, from school days, with a wife. And they worked in the kitchen, so we had enough food already, you know. We didn't stay too long. In the meantime, Mary's father, my wife's father, were looking for the kids. They went to Salzburg. They took-- they left them off in Salzburg, so he didn't stay. He has to find the kids. And he was traveling. I don't know how he found us. One morning, somebody calls, Mania, Mania! That was her father. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with Man-- with Mania Mary--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the same kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same kibbutz, yeah. We weren't married at that time, but I-- we were close, you know. We were holding, you know, together. So I went with them, you know. And going back to Salzburg on a station, you know, we found her little brother. He got mad at the kibbutz. They didn't treat him nice. So he was running away. He was a little boy. He was about 12 years old, you know. He ran away. Where was he going? We found him on a station.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we picked him up, and we took him with us to Salzburg. From Salzburg, we went to Germany. Where, where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed, we stayed a little bit there in a camp, in a DP camp named Bialik. There I found my, my cousin, you know, from Lodz that I, I saw. They were-- they left already, too, you know. They stayed there. So but we left. We didn't wait. They came later. We went to [NON-ENGLISH]. Was a little camp that used to be a camp for the Hitler Youth, you know, because he had the militaristic ideas, you know, even for the youth. As a matter of fact, later on, they, they send them on the front line, you know, to fight. They, they send, what, 15-, 16-year-olds, 14-year-olds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a camp for the Hitler Youth. You know, it was a camp barracks, wooden barracks, you know. And we were staying there. And we didn't know what the, the next day is going to bring, you know. You couldn't settle down. We didn't want to settle down in Germany. We wanted to go to Israel. What year was this by then? Was this still '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '46. That was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll stop this tape and start another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
